aliases: Chicken breast roasted with skin on

english: Chicken breast roasted with skin on

ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, breast, with skin, roasted
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for roasted chicken breast with skin; SNOMED CT coverage for prepared poultry cuts is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is one medium roasted chicken breast with skin, approximately 130 g, providing around 239 kcal, 35.7 g protein, and 10.6 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, with skin, cooked, roasted — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (F11010005) and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 331960 / NDB No. 05013)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, calcium, iron) sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database, record F11010005, an approved primary regional source with confidence 0.95. Supplementary micronutrient and fatty acid data (saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, potassium, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 331960 (NDB No. 05013), 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, with skin, cooked, roasted', an approved secondary global source. Values are consistent across both sources for overlapping nutrients.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index not applicable — negligible carbohydrate content confirmed via USDA FoodData Central and HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
gi_source.organization: USDA FoodData Central; Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore
gi_source.source_match_notes: Roasted chicken breast with skin contains only 0.02 g carbohydrate per 100 g as confirmed by both HPB Singapore (F11010005) and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 331960). Glycemic index measurement requires a minimum available carbohydrate load of 25–50 g; this food cannot elicit a meaningful glycaemic response. GI is therefore not applicable and is set to null per Veritas critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0. No GI measurement exists or is needed for this food in the University of Sydney GI Database.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 347 mg/100 g is in the borderline range (200–400 mg/100 g); potassium at ~245 mg/100 g partially offsets the sodium load.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100–130 g, avoid adding high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce, and consider removing the skin to reduce total fat and caloric density, thereby supporting overall cardiovascular blood pressure management.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (0.02 g/100 g) and high protein (27.48 g/100 g) result in no meaningful glycaemic impact.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Roasted chicken breast with skin is an excellent protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the near-zero carbohydrate content produces no glycaemic response, and the high protein content supports satiety and lean muscle maintenance — however, skin should be removed to limit saturated fat intake and support overall metabolic health.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 8.18 g/100 g with saturated fat ~2.30 g/100 g; sodium 347 mg/100 g is borderline for cardiovascular patients.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, removing the skin reduces saturated fat to approximately 0.9 g/100 g and total fat to approximately 3.6 g/100 g, making skinless roasted chicken breast a heart-healthy lean protein; if skin is retained, portion control to 100 g and avoidance of high-sodium sauces is strongly advised.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at ~2.30 g/100 g; no trans fat; high protein supports tissue repair; no significant anti-inflammatory nutrients present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Roasted chicken breast with skin is acceptable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to its low saturated fat and absence of trans fat; removing the skin is recommended to further reduce pro-inflammatory saturated fat, and pairing with omega-3-rich foods or anti-inflammatory vegetables is advised to compensate for the absence of intrinsic anti-inflammatory factors.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted chicken breast with skin is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked poultry product prepared by dry-heat oven roasting at temperatures typically between 180–220°C until the internal temperature reaches 74°C. The skin is retained during cooking, contributing additional fat, flavour, and a characteristic golden-brown, slightly crispy exterior texture. The meat itself is firm, moist when properly cooked, and has a mild savoury flavour with subtle roasted notes. Per 100 g, it provides 184 kcal, 27.48 g protein, 8.18 g total fat (of which approximately 2.30 g is saturated fat), and only 0.02 g carbohydrate, making it effectively carbohydrate-free. Sodium is 347 mg/100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management. It is an excellent source of niacin (B3), vitamin B6, selenium, and phosphorus, and a good source of zinc and vitamin B12. The glycaemic index is not applicable due to negligible carbohydrate content. Clinically, this food is highly suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management and weight-loss diets due to its high protein density and near-zero carbohydrate profile. For cardiovascular and hypertension patients, removing the skin is recommended to reduce saturated fat and total fat. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal when sourced from halal-certified suppliers. Commonly served as a main protein component in Western meals, meal-prep bowls, salads, and sandwiches.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values primarily sourced from HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (cr_id F11010005, confidence 0.95), which is an approved primary regional source. Supplementary micronutrient and fatty acid data from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 331960, NDB No. 05013) for 'Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, with skin, cooked, roasted', also an approved secondary source. Values are consistent across both sources for overlapping nutrients.
notes: Carbohydrate_g is 0.02 g/100 g — this is a confirmed near-zero trace value reported by HPB Singapore (F11010005); it is nutritionally negligible and produces no glycaemic response. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB; chicken breast contains no dietary fibre (naturally zero). Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 331960) for roasted chicken breast with skin. Saturated_fat_g (2.30 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (3.21 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.77 g), cholesterol_mg (84.0 mg), potassium_mg (245.0 mg), and all micronutrient values not reported by HPB were sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 331960 (Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, with skin, cooked, roasted), which is an approved secondary source. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0.02 g/100 g); GI set to null and gi_category set to not_applicable per Veritas critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods. gi_source references USDA FoodData Central and HPB as the approved sources confirming negligible carbohydrate content. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is a generic Western/international preparation — therefore no mandatory non-English local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. Halal status is conditional on sourcing from halal-certified suppliers.

Nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore (primary approved source, F11010005) with supplementary micronutrients from USDA FoodData Central (approved secondary source, FDC ID 331960). nutrient_source.organization correctly identifies HPB as the primary approved source. gi_source.organization correctly references USDA FoodData Central and HPB as approved sources confirming negligible carbohydrate content, resolving the g2 failure from iteration 2. glycemic_index is null (not 0) with gi_category 'not_applicable', resolving the q4 failure from iteration 2. All three mandatory health conditions present with specific clinical guidance. Ontology anchors are defensible. LLM description exceeds 200 characters.
